## Excercises on basic concepts of heat transfer {#sec-basicHTx}

The excersises are created in three levels. The first level consists of exercises to train and automate basic calculations. This level can be revisited at anytime to tune calculational skills. The second level consists of exersises where the skills from the first level needs to connected in order to solve basic fire engineering problems. The third level includes all from the second level but demands more with regards to connecting first- and second level skills. As a complement to these exercises, interactive exercises will be lauched.

### First level skills

#### Steady state conduction

A wall consists of three layers according to table [-@tbl-bht1]

| $d_i$ [mm]  | Material         | $\lambda_i$ [W/mK] |
|:-----:|:-----------------|:-------:|
| 20    | Wood board       | 0.14    |
| 100   | Fibre insulation | 0.04    |
| 20    | Gypsum board     | 0.5     |
: {#tbl-bht1}

The temperature on the outer face of the wooden board has a constant temperature of 75ÂḞC. The opposing side of the wall has a temperature of 15ÂḞC, Calculate the temperaturs at the insulations interface surfaces $T_1$ and $T_2$.

**Solution**

By calculating the total thermal resistance of the wall, $R_{tot}$, it is possible to find intermidiate temperatures using the equation

$$
T_i = T_0 + \frac{\sum_{j = 0}^{i} R_j}{R_{tot}} \left(T_n - T_0\right)
$$

$R_{tot}$ is the sum of each of the layers as

$$
R_{tot} = \sum_{j=0}^{i} R_j = R_{wood} + R_{ins} + R_{gyp} = \frac{l_{wood}}{\lambda_{wood}} + \frac{l_{ins}}{\lambda_{ins}} + \frac{l_{gyp}}{\lambda_{gyp}} = \frac{0.02}{0.14} + \frac{0.1}{0.04} + \frac{0.12}{0.5} = 2.67 \text{ } mÂĠK/W
$$

As $T_0$ = 75ÂḞC and $T_n$ = 15ÂḞC, the temperatures at each layer interface, $T_1$ and $T_2$ can be calculated as

$$
T_1 = T_0 + \frac{R_{wood}}{R_{tot}} \left(T_n - T_0 \right) = 
75 + \frac{\tfrac{0.02}{0.14}}{2.67} \left(15 - 75 \right) = 71.8ÂḞC
$$

and 

$$
T_2 = T_0 + \frac{R_{wood} + R_{ins}}{R_{tot}} \left(T_n - T_0 \right) = 
75 + \frac{\tfrac{0.02}{0.14} + \tfrac{0.1}{0.04}}{2.67} \left(15 - 75 \right) = 15.6ÂḞC
$$

#### Surface temperature

Calculate the surface temperature, $T_1$.

| $d_i$ [mm]  | Material         | $\lambda_i$ [W/mK] |
|:-----:|:-----------------|:-------:|
| 12    | Wood board       | 0.2    |
: {#tbl-bht2}

Assume the heat transfer coefficient, $h_{tot}$, equal to 5 W/mÂĠK for the exposed side and a constant temperature on the unexposed side, $T_2$, equal to 20ÂḞC.

**Solution**

By calculating the total thermal resistance of the wall, $R_{tot}$, it is possible to find intermidiate temperatures using the equation

$$
T_i = T_0 + \frac{\sum_{j = 0}^{i} R_j}{R_{tot}} \left(T_n - T_0\right)
$$

In this case, $T_0$ = $T_g$, $T_n$ = $T_2$, and $R_{tot}$ is the total resistance of the wall including the heat transfer coefficient on the exposed side.

$$
R_{tot} = R_{h} + R_{k} = \frac{1}{h_{tot}} + \frac{l_{wood}}{\lambda_{wood}} = \frac{0.1}{5} + \frac{0.02}{0.14} = 0.26 \text{ } mÂĠK/W
$$

As $T_g$ = 100ÂḞC and $T_2$ = 20ÂḞC, the temperatures at the surface, $T_1$, can be calculated as

$$
T_1 = T_g + \frac{R_{h}}{R_{tot}} \left(T_2 - T_g \right) = 
75 + \frac{\tfrac{1}{5}}{0.26} \left(20 - 100 \right) = 38.5ÂḞC
$$

An alternative way to calculate the surface temperature is by using

$$
T_1 = \frac{R_h T_2 + R_k T_g}{R_{h} + R_k} = 38.5ÂḞC
$$

Both solutions yield the same result and are proper to use.
